talk_title: Chromatin redistribution of Rb reveals estrogen‑response activation by CDK4/6 inhibition

models.name: Patients treated with CDK4/6 inhibitor monotherapy

clinical_data.n: n=3 cell lines, n=2 PDX, n=patients (paired)

MOA_summary: CDK4/6 inhibition dephosphorylates Rb, causing widespread chromatin binding; beyond canonical E2F repression, Rb is recruited to active enhancers of estrogen‑responsive genes, enhancing their expression (including cyclin D1), which can blunt cell‑cycle arrest unless blocked by endocrine therapy.
key_findings: CDK4/6 inhibition increases chromatin‑bound Rb at >10,000 sites, 75% at promoters and many at active enhancers.
Rb binding at non‑promoter enhancers is associated with increased H3K27ac and up‑regulation of nearby estrogen‑response genes.
CRISPR disruption of Rb‑bound enhancers attenuates estrogen‑response gene induction and cyclin D1 protein up‑regulation.
Combining CDK4/6 inhibitors with endocrine therapy yields deeper suppression of cell‑cycle genes and prevents cyclin D1‑mediated resistance.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Rb‑driven estrogen response may limit monotherapy efficacy and contribute to resistance in ESR1‑mutant tumors.

combination_strategies.rationale: Blocks Rb‑induced estrogen‑response program that counteracts cell‑cycle arrest
combination_strategies.phenotype: Deeper suppression of E2F targets and reduced cyclin D1 levels

open_questions: Which E2F family members are recruited by Rb at non‑cell‑cycle sites?
Can selective Rb mutants separate E2F repression from estrogen‑response activation?

talk_title: Programmable RNA sensors for cancer‑specific fusion targeting
MOA_summary: ADAR‑mediated RNA editing sensors are programmed to bind cancer‑specific fusion breakpoints; upon editing a stop‑codon in the guide, translation of a downstream cytotoxic payload (e.g., iCaspase9) is enabled, selectively killing fusion‑positive cells.
key_findings: Linear and gap RNA sensors selectively activate reporter expression in cells harboring the target fusion RNA.
In MCF7 cells with an endogenous ARFGAP2‑SOX2 fusion, gap sensors drive mNeon fluorescence with >10‑fold specificity over fusion‑negative MCF10A.
Fusion‑targeted sensors coupled to iCaspase9 reduce MCF7 viability to ~80% versus ~100% for non‑targeting controls.
High‑throughput sensor library screening begins to define design rules for effective RNA‑sensor constructs.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Variable sensor performance suggests sequence‑context dependent efficacy; loss or mutation of the fusion breakpoint could confer resistance.
open_questions: What are the optimal payloads (immune‑stimulating, secreted factors) for in‑vivo efficacy?
How can delivery be achieved at scale (LNP, viral nanoparticles) to reach tumor cells in patients?
